sentences.text: Jackson.
And at this point, he and all of his brothers are still alive, and he has come to Debrecen.
In-- in Debrecen, my brother managed to find a-- a place for me that it-- it was a Gentile neighborhood, in a Gentile neighborhood, to rent a place for me.
Because the-- the Jewish neighborhoods were constantly [? razia. ?] The Hungarian [? razia-- ?]
Raids.
They would block up two, three blocks, close them up, and, and, and search all over.
And, and actually what they were doing, they were looking for people like, like us that had no-- had no papers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in this place here, I managed to get a very good place that was a Gentile neighborhood, and very seldom that they made [? razias ?] in that area.
And the owner was a-- the owner of the house was a World War I Hungarian veteran, and an invalid, a hero of World War I.
And that plaque was on the, on the door of the gate.
And usually, the police or gendarmes or whatever, they wouldn't come in into this place.
So I was hiding out in this-- this place.
And at that time, at that time already I remember I started to record the, the, the trace back from the days we left Torun to write a diary.
But it got lost.
But I remember that's what I-- that's what I was doing already then.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] It dragged on.
It dragged on in Debrecen.
This was May, 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We were in Debrecen another-- till October, November.
And in Debrecen, I was.
And my brother [NON-ENGLISH] was in [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was in [NON-ENGLISH].
And I have to mention that my brother [NON-ENGLISH] wife and her little baby was home.
But they were afraid.
They were afraid to-- to communicate.
So he was back.
And he didn't see his baby for another year.
As a matter of fact, he didn't see her till sometime in November or December of 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]
I took chances in 19-- when my brother [NON-ENGLISH] was already in Satu Mare, I took chances from Debrecen.
It was a three hour train ride.
I took the chance twice to take the train, and go down and meet him.
And the only-- the only real help that we gave him-- being together all this time didn't give him any help at all.
Maybe he felt a little bit more secure.
The only real help that we gave him was that we crossed first.
And we were able to let our parents know where he is.
So they were able to send-- otherwise, you can't send in a place somebody you don't know where.
That That was the real help that we gave him.
And a little later, I think in November, he left-- he left-- he meaning [NON-ENGLISH], left Satu Mare.
And he went to a city called [NON-ENGLISH].
For what reason he went there, I think-- I think he managed to get some kind of a job over there.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This dragged on-- I-- I have to-- I have to-- I think I'm going to skip this here, because it's taking too long.
With all this time, my parents were spending a fortune to legalize-- to get us the Hungarian citizenship.
And he had a lawyer in Budapest, which cost a fortune.
And we finally did get this citizenship, because they couldn't deny it.
All they wanted is 90 years to show that we lived in this place.
And it was more than 100, 120 years.
My great grandfathers were in there.
So-- but this was a way of squeezing out the last Jews.
When I finally the-- the paper, they wanted to send it to Torun, officially.
In Torun, we couldn't-- we couldn't go back.
So my cousin in Mariapocs managed-- gave us an idea.
In Debrecen, I found out-- I believe I mentioned that already, but I'll repeat it again, that my brother [NON-ENGLISH] was being brought over.
And he was saved, and sent to Satu Mare.
And my father had-- father and mother had to leave Torun and become a refugee, and live in [NON-ENGLISH] with their-- with his sister, sister and brother-in-law.
Sometimes in-- in October or November, we got a notification from the lawyer that we can get-- we will get the citizenship paper.
My brother Eliezer was supposedly the legal one, because he was not deported on the other side.
He traveled to Budapest.
And they insisted-- and they insisted that they can-- they have to send it registered to Torun.
They cannot give it to the lawyer.
They cannot give it out to the per-- It has to go through-- registered to an office.
So this was a big obstacle.
We couldn't go to Torun.
We knew that this is going into the lion's-- lion's den, or lion's mouth.
But my cousin in Mariapocs, he had an idea.
He said, maybe if you convince the lawyer, he should convince the authorities in Budapest, that you are living now-- [NON-ENGLISH] was living already in Mariapocs.
Then I come over to Mariapocs.
And they send it directly to the notary in there officially.
And he will have influence that we will be able to get it over there.
And that's exactly-- that's exactly what happened.
It cost some more money.
You had to-- you had to grease the wheels all the time.
We got some more money.
We got to grease the wheels to get the paper.
We finally send it out to Mariapocs.
And I traveled over there too.
And we did get the paper.
So we have the citizenship paper, like any other Hungarian Jew.
So our [NON-ENGLISH] is-- is the same.
That's what I thought.
But it didn't work-- it didn't work out.
What is [NON-ENGLISH]?
[NON-ENGLISH] is destiny.
[NON-ENGLISH] is the Hebrew word for destiny.
We share the same destiny.
But, of course, I didn't see my mother since August, 1941.
And here, we are in October of 1942.
So I decided, I'll take a chance.
And I'll go to see my parents.
They weren't home anymore.
No Torun, I don't have to go to Torun.
I go to, [NON-ENGLISH], where they are living.
And what I did was I made a copy of my citizenship papers.
I made two copies.
But at that time, you know, they didn't have, like you put in a copying machine and you make one, two, three.
What copies used to be done in those days was you went to a photographer.
And he had some four thumbtacks, and made tagged it on on a table.
Then he took a photograph of it.
And that's how you had to-- I made two of them.
I left one in Debrecen.
And I'm armed with the citizenship paper.
And I'm traveling to see my mother.
When I got to [NON-ENGLISH] by the bus, the-- the detective stopped me.
And-- and said-- people that walked out of the bus, he stopped a few people and for papers.
And I showed them that I have the paper.
But I was suspicious for him.
And he said, come with me.
So he took me up-- he took me-- took me up into a building on the Main Street, not too far from where my uncle and my mother and father was there.
They lived in one room that they had there.
It was a two-story building.
They put me up on the second floor.
And I took me into an office.
And I sat down.
Said, sit down, in front of his desk.
And then he pulls out the paper from the desk.
And he says, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] you know tell me the truth, he says.
How did you get this citizenship paper?
You were deported on the other side in-- in the Ukraine, in Poland.
Of course, I started to deny.
I wasn't.
I lived in Mariapocs.
And that's where I got my citizenship paper.
What saved me that time, I think, was that he said to me-- he noticed that there were four tiny holes at each corner of the citizenship paper.
So he noticed that-- did you make a copy of that citizenship?
I said, yes.
He said, where is that copy?
I said, I left it in Mariapocs.
I left one with my brother, and with my-- anyway, they started to beat me up.
He called on another guy.
And he pulled out a rubber stick, made out of rubber.
And he started to hit me over the head that I became bloody all over the face.
And I-- I fell off the chair.
And he called another detective in.
And he started kicking me in the head, kicking me in the side.
Now, tell me the truth.
Were you deported to Poland?
I said, no, I wasn't deported.
Anyway, I'll make this story short.
I realized that time that either I'm going to tell them that I was.
Or they're going to knock my brains out.
And they'll kill me right here.
When I told them-- when I told them that I-- that I was, then I got additional-- additional-- I was bloody completely my-- from my ears were-- were-- blood was coming out of my mouth.
And after that-- that was the worst beating I ever got in my life.
And this was a two-story building.
But they had no elevator.
You had steps.
Then the two guys grabbed me.
I had a hat on.
The hat was left there.
And they opened the door.
And they gave me a shove.
And I flew with my head down all the way, and get the hell out of here, in Hungarian.
How-- how I got out up, and how-- I don't know.
When you are 21 years, or 22 years old, you muster all the [NON-ENGLISH] that you can.
When I-- and I came-- and I came-- that wasn't too far on the main street to-- to my parents.
When I came in, and my parents saw me, first of all, they-- they-- they didn't want me to come.
They didn't.
They were afraid.
They were afraid, all right.
When I saw my mother was in hysterics, my father-- they didn't know.
Anyway, they-- my mother was-- was putting we towels on me.
And I was-- I couldn't see out, because I was puffed up.
All night, I was so sorry that I-- I-- instead of-- instead of giving them-- giving them pleasure and joy at seeing me, I made them-- I made them to see these stories.
The next morning, the next day [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a guy by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. he had a printing business.
And my father was friendly with him.
And he had a daughter.
And this daughter, through him, she was the one that had connections with the police that hired this detective that brought us over.
Everything went for money, for-- you know, to grease the-- so when my father went-- first of all, they took my citizenship paper away.
I had no more paper.
So when my father went over to this Steinberg.
And he said, maybe we can do something.
And he talked it over with his daughter.
And his daughter talked to other people.
And she said, I'll be able to-- I'll be able to get the citizenship paper back to you.
But you better get out of here.
Don't-- don't stay here.
Another day or two-- another day or two, I got my-- they got my citizenship paper.
They probably wouldn't have given it to me if they wouldn't have known that I have a copy of it.
And-- and I left that time.
That was my joy of coming to see my-- my parents.
When I went back, I went back to Debrecen, I didn't go to see my brother, because my sister-in-law, I didn't want them to see the way I looked.
I was black and blue all over.

It takes a couple of weeks to-- but I didn't wait completely two weeks till when I came-- when I came for the first time back to see my sister-in-law, my brother's [NON-ENGLISH] wife, [NON-ENGLISH] was her name, she started to cry when she saw-- when she saw me first, when I told her what happened.
Anyway, this was my experience with becoming a Hungarian citizen again.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was in November.
In January or February, 1943 [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we got a note from my mother-- my brother [NON-ENGLISH] who was the [INAUDIBLE], that my father is very sick.
And my mother is taking him to the city of [?
Khost, ?] because in [NON-ENGLISH] there was-- was a couple doctors, but just emergency rooms, but no hospital.
To a doctor by the name of [NON-ENGLISH].
He is coughing badly.
And she's taking him to the doctor.
And she kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sensed something.
And she said that we should come-- we should come to the city of [?
Khost ?] and see what's-- what-- so sometimes in January, 1943, we came to the city of [?
Khost. ?] And that didn't go-- didn't go so easy.
We got off-- we got off the train station, we got stopped, rounded up.
My brother [NON-ENGLISH] was in [NON-ENGLISH].
And only the three of us, [NON-ENGLISH], and I went in.
They rounded us up.
And they took us into a barracks in the city of [?
Khost ?] already.
But, fortunately, we had-- we had the papers.
And we were rounded up for-- held back.
And they checked out the papers.
And they said, what are you all doing here?
We told them the truth.
Our father is here for medical care.
And that's why we came-- we came to see him.
And they let us out.
But it was a few hours.
In meantime, my parents were in a-- in a-- people that they knew in a bed and breakfast place that they stayed.
And the whole city knew already that they rounded up so many people in-- from the station.
They knew that we were rounded up again, again these-- but that didn't last an-- ah.
One incident that I have to mention is that when they rounded us up, among us was one Jew from Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he couldn't speak a word of English, had no paper whatsoever.
He escaped somehow from Poland.
And he was there.
And he said to me, oh my God, what am I going to do?
What shall I say?
And this guy took a dash to the door.
There was one gendarme, or policeman, I don't remember exactly what-- he was in that room watching us.
And then they would call one by one, one by one.
And then they would let us out.
This guy dash to the door, and out.
When he saw the door closed, he ran after him, and two or three shots rang out.
I don't know what happened, if he got hit or not, or if he escaped, or he was dead.
I don't know.
But, anyway, that's what happened then.
So my father went to the-- to the-- to this doctor [NON-ENGLISH].
And he-- he-- they have [NON-ENGLISH] in German.
It's a [NON-ENGLISH] machine, that you look into the lungs.
And you can look inside.
And the doctors said, he was-- he was a very heavy smoker, my father.
So he didn't give us too much-- too much encouragement.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my father, evidently he-- he knew what was going on.
He had a certain-- certain piece of forest that was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And he held that, I remember, in the '30s, when my mother wanted him to-- the depression was-- we were tight in money.
She told him, why don't you sell this here?
Let's not squeeze ourselves.
He says, it's too young.
It needs another 10 years, another 10, 15 years.
It's too young to cut yet.
But I-- already then, I think he realized what the situation was.
He had this [NON-ENGLISH] that he lived that time in [?
Khost. ?] And he knew-- and he knew what the situation was.
I guess he wanted us to be there.
And he called us.
He says, let's go over to him.
He told them that, you can go and cut this part of forest, but to give him a down payment.
And he gave him in front of us-- I guess he wanted us-- because, already, that was in '43.
Already, we thought that the war is going to end soon.
And he gave him 10,000 [INAUDIBLE], which was a lot of money that time, as a down payment.
And he said, when you cut it all up, and you measure it all out, and you tell me.
Whatever you'll say, you owe me, I'll accept it.
But then my brother, my oldest brother, said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were talking among themselves.
He said, I have-- I would like to take Dad to Debrecen.
Debrecen is a larger city.
That is more doctors, more hospitals.
And-- and when I think about it today, he had no way of communicating with his wife.
He himself lived in an apartment that was two rooms with his two children-- two rooms, a kitchen and a room.
A little foyer was there.
Without-- without even notifying, he said, Dad, I want you to come to Debrecen.
And he took us-- he took us to Debrecen.
And we all went back with him to Debrecen.
OK.
